topic: trying to learn Arabic on Duolingo — funny mistakes

turns.content: quick question — is Arabic actually the hardest language to learn or does it just FEEL that way because I'm terrible at it haha

Arabic is consistently ranked among the hardest languages for speakers of European languages — the US State Department puts it in Category IV, their highest difficulty rating, with an estimated 2,200 hours to reach proficiency. So it's not just you! What's giving you the most trouble — the script, the sounds, the grammar?

ALL OF IT haha but honestly the sounds are killing me. There are sounds in Arabic that my mouth just does not know how to make. Like the 'ayn sound?? I've been trying to do it and my colleague at work was like "Bruna that sounds like you're choking" and I was dying laughing. My Portuguese mouth was not designed for these sounds gente

The 'ayn is notoriously difficult for non-Arabic speakers — it's a pharyngeal consonant, meaning it's produced deep in the throat in a way that most European languages simply don't use. Portuguese has a lot of interesting sounds (the nasal vowels, the soft 'r'), but nothing that maps to Arabic pharyngeals. A good trick: try yawning and making a sound at the deepest point of the yawn — that throat constriction is roughly where 'ayn lives. Are you learning Modern Standard Arabic or Gulf Arabic dialect?

I'm doing Modern Standard on Duolingo but honestly I think I need Gulf dialect more for daily life because that's what people actually speak around me. The Duolingo course is fine for basics but some of the sentences they teach you are SO random. Yesterday it made me say "the elephant is in the kitchen" in Arabic. When am I ever going to need that?? Haha

The Duolingo random sentence phenomenon is legendary — "the elephant is in the kitchen" is peak Duolingo energy. For practical daily life, you're right that Gulf Arabic would be more immediately useful. MSA is great for reading and formal contexts, but locals and daily interactions use the regional dialect. Some resources for Gulf Arabic specifically: the Kalaam app focuses on Gulf dialect, and there are some good YouTube channels like Arabic with Sam that teach conversational Gulf Arabic. How long have you been at it?

I started about two months ago and I have a 47-day streak on Duolingo which I am very proud of haha! I can say basic things now — hello, how are you, thank you, numbers, some food words. But then I try to use what I learned in real life and people respond in rapid-fire Arabic and I just stand there smiling and nodding like a bobblehead. The gap between Duolingo and real conversation is HUGE

A 47-day streak is genuinely impressive — consistency matters more than anything in language learning, so you're building good habits. And that gap between app learning and real conversation is completely normal. Apps are great for vocabulary and basic patterns, but they can't replicate the speed, slang, and unpredictability of real speech. A few things that can help bridge the gap: try listening to Gulf Arabic podcasts or YouTube content just for exposure, even if you only catch 10% of the words. Your ear needs to get used to natural speech patterns and speed. Also, if there's anyone at work or in your social circle who'd be willing to have short, simple Arabic-only conversations with you, that practice is worth more than 100 Duolingo lessons.

our receptionist at work is Emirati and she's been so sweet about helping me! She teaches me a new phrase every day but then she laughs because my accent is apparently hilarious. She says I make Arabic sound like Portuguese haha. I keep putting the emphasis on the wrong syllables. And some of the letter sounds I literally cannot distinguish — like there are like four different versions of what sounds like 's' to me??

Your receptionist sounds like the perfect informal tutor! And yes, Arabic has multiple 's'-like sounds that sound identical to non-native ears at first: there's the regular 'sin,' the emphatic 'sad,' and then 'tha' which is more like 'th' but gets confused too. The emphatic consonants (sad, dad, ta, dha) are produced with the tongue pressed against the roof of the mouth, which gives them a heavier, darker sound. The distinction is really important because it can change the meaning of a word entirely — 'sara' and 'saara' with different s-sounds mean completely different things. The good news: your ear will start distinguishing them with exposure. It just takes time.

ai meu Deus FOUR different s-sounds!! No wonder I'm confused. Okay but I'm not giving up because I think it would be so cool to actually speak Arabic even at a basic conversational level. Plus people here get SO happy when you try to speak their language, even badly. I said shukran to a taxi driver the other day and he was so pleased he gave me a whole Arabic lesson for the rest of the ride haha. I love that

That taxi driver reaction is really common in the Gulf — people genuinely appreciate the effort because many expats never bother to learn even basic Arabic. That goodwill is a huge advantage: you'll get free practice and encouragement everywhere you go. Keep the Duolingo streak alive for the structure, practice with your receptionist for the real-world application, and don't stress about the accent. Embrace sounding Portuguese-Arabic — it's charming and people clearly love it.

Portuguese-Arabic, a new dialect haha!! Okay I'm going to keep going. My goal is to have a real conversation by the end of the year, even a simple one. Wish me luck querida! And thank you for the 'ayn yawning tip, I'm going to try that right now haha

Good luck with the 'ayn practice — just maybe don't do it at work or people will think you're very tired! Your end-of-year goal is totally achievable with your streak and your receptionist as a tutor. Keep at it!

hahaha too late, I just tried it and my colleague across the room gave me the most concerned look!! He was like 'Bruna are you okay??' and I had to explain I was practicing Arabic sounds. Nossa the things I do for language learning haha. Okay I'm going back to my patients before I get fired for making strange noises at work. Obrigada meu bem!!

Your colleague is going to get very used to strange sounds coming from your direction if you keep this up! Good luck with the Arabic journey — the fact that you're willing to embarrass yourself for it shows real commitment. Talk soon!
